title: Methodological Framework
text:
Collaboration Model
This initiative is designed to be a distributed and inclusive consortium.

An open call for participation to research groups worldwide.
Clear guidelines on data ownership, authorship, and acknowledgment.
Opportunities for co-authored publications and thematic working groups.
Regular communication through virtual meetings, workshops, and shared online platforms.

Participation levels are flexible, enabling contributors to engage based on their resources and interests.
Beyond initial data generation, the initiative aims to:

Serve as a “living genomic resource”, continuously expanded with new samples and data.
Integrate genomic information with ecological, climatic, and distributional data.
Support training and capacity building, particularly in regions heavily affected by the invasion.
Provide a model framework applicable to other invasive species.

title: Methodological Framework
text:
Sampling Strategy
A coordinated sampling strategy will aim to cover:

The native range of the species.
Multiple invaded regions across continents.
Diverse environmental and ecological conditions (multiple habitats).

To ensure data comparability, contributors will be encouraged to follow standardized sampling and preservation protocols. They will also be provided with detailed guidelines for tissue collection, DNA extraction, and storage.

title: Methodological Framework
text:
Genomic Data Generation
The core genomic approach will be whole-genome sequencing (WGS) using either short- or long-read technologies. Depending on the available resources, the following sequencing strategies may be employed:

Resequencing of multiple individuals across populations.
Hybrid assemblies combining short and long reads.

title: Methodological Framework
text:
Data Management and Curation
All genomic data will be: - Stored in a centralized database with standardized formats.

Linked to comprehensive metadata records.
Submitted, when possible, to public repositories (e.g., international nucleotide sequence databases), following FAIR data principles (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable).

Quality control pipelines and documentation will be developed to ensure the reliability and reproducibility of the data.

title: Participation and Data Contribution
text:
To achieve broad geographic and ecological coverage, this project invites researchers to contribute plant tissue samples, extracted DNA, and/or whole-genome sequencing data. The project is designed to be flexible, allowing contributors to participate according to their available resources while adhering to shared standards that ensure data comparability and reproducibility.
Contributors may participate by:

sending plant tissues preserved in silica gel,
sending extracted genomic DNA, and/or
sharing whole-genome sequencing data generated independently.

title: Participation and Data Contribution
text:
Initial contact
Contact the coordinating team to express interest and indicate the type of contribution (plant tissue, DNA, and/or genomic data).

title: Participation and Data Contribution
text:
Sampling and preservation

Collect young, healthy leaf tissue when possible.
Preserve tissue immediately in silica gel until fully desiccated.
Clearly label each sample with a unique identifier.

title: Participation and Data Contribution
text:
Metadata recording
Record standardized metadata for each sample, including (at minimum): - Geographic coordinates and locality description - Habitat and environmental context - Sampling date and collector - Preservation method

title: Participation and Data Contribution
text:
DNA extraction or sequencing
Contributors may extract genomic DNA the provided guidelines, orPerform whole-genome sequencing locally using short- or long-read technologies.

title: Participation and Data Contribution
text:
Sample or data submission
Ship silica-dried tissues or extracted DNA provided instructions, or share raw sequencing reads and associated metadata through the designated data-transfer platform.

title: Participation and Data Contribution
text:
Data integration and acknowledgment
All contributions will be curated centrally, integrated into downstream analyses, and appropriately acknowledged through co-authorship or formal data citation, depending on the level of contribution.

title: International Water Hyacinth Genomics Initiative
text:
Objectives

Generate a global genomic database of Pontederia crassipes (Water Hyacinth) using whole-genome sequencing and complementary genomic approaches.
Characterize genetic diversity and population structure of P. crassipes across the native and invaded ranges.
Reconstruct invasion routes and dispersal pathways of P. crassipes at regional and global scales.
Identify genomic regions associated with adaptation of P. crassipes, invasiveness, and environmental tolerance.
Promote international collaboration and data sharing among P. crassipes researchers, institutions, and management agencies.

title: International Water Hyacinth Genomics Initiative
text:
Scope of the Initiative
The initiative is an open and collaborative framework designed to welcome contributions from researchers worldwide. Participation may include:

Providing plant tissue or DNA samples from well-documented populations.
Contributing raw whole-genome sequencing data or assembled genomes.
Sharing associated metadata, including geographic location, habitat type, sampling date, and ecological context.
Participating in joint analyses, publications, and related activities.

title: Guides and Protocols
text:
In our laboratory, we utilize standardized protocols for the extraction and quality control quantification of nucleic acids. Below we outline the step-by-step procedures and equipment employed.





This protocol is optimized for fresh or frozen plant tissue. (Note: We have adjusted the homogenization and centrifugation parameters to adapt them to our specific laboratory equipment).
Preparation:

Add 5–100 mg of fresh or frozen plant tissue and 500 µl of Solution CD1 into a 2 ml tissue disruption tube. Vortex briefly to mix. (Note: If your sample is high in phenolic compounds, add 450 µl of Solution CD1 and 50 µl of Solution PS).

Steps:

Homogenization: Secure the tissue disruption tubes onto a Vortex Adapter. Vortex at maximum speed between 10–15 for 3 to 5 minutes.
Centrifuge the tissue disruption tubes at 12,000 x g (~11,300 rpm) for 2 min.
Transfer the supernatant to a clean 1.5 ml microcentrifuge tube. (Expect 350–450 µl. The supernatant may still contain some plant particles).
Add 200 µl of Solution CD2 and vortex for 5 s.
Centrifuge at 12,000 x g for 1 min at room temperature. Avoiding the pellet, transfer the supernatant to a clean 1.5 ml microcentrifuge tube. (Expect 400–500 µl).
Add 500 µl of Buffer APP and vortex for 5 s.
Load 600 µl of lysate onto an MB Spin Column. Centrifuge at 12,000 x g for 1 min. Discard the flow-through and repeat this step to ensure that all of the lysate has passed through the MB spin column.
Place the MB spin column into a clean 2 ml collection tube. Add 650 µl of Buffer AW1 to the MB spin column. Centrifuge at 12,000 x g for 1 min. Discard the flow-through and place the MB spin column back into the same 2 ml collection tube.
Add 650 µl of Buffer AW2 to the MB spin column. Centrifuge at 12,000 x g for 1 min. Discard the flow-through and place the MB spin column into a new 1.5 ml elution tube.
Centrifuge at up to 16,000 x g (~13,000 rpm) for 2 min to completely dry the membrane.
Elution: Add 50–100 µl of Buffer EB to the center of the white filter membrane. Let it sit briefly, then centrifuge at 12,000 x g for 1 min. The DNA is now ready for downstream applications.

🔗 Official QIAGEN Plant Pro Handbook



Used for the purification of total DNA from animal tissues, insects, bacteria, or other materials.
Summary Steps for Purification from Tissue:

Cut tissue (≤25 mg animal tissue or insects) into small pieces, and place in a 1.5 ml microcentrifuge tube.
Add 180 µl of Buffer ATL and 20 µl of proteinase K. Mix by vortexing and incubate at 56°C until completely lysed. Vortex occasionally during incubation. Vortex 15 s directly before proceeding to the next step.
Add 200 µl of Buffer AL. Mix thoroughly by vortexing. Incubate at 56°C for 10 min.
Add 200 µl of ethanol (96–100%). Mix thoroughly by vortexing.
Pipet the mixture into a DNeasy Mini spin column placed in a 2 ml collection tube. Centrifuge at ≥6000 x g (8000 rpm) for 1 min. Discard the flow-through and collection tube.
Place the spin column in a new 2 ml collection tube. Add 500 µl of Buffer AW1. Centrifuge for 1 min at ≥6000 x g. Discard the flow-through and collection tube.
Place the spin column in a new 2 ml collection tube. Add 500 µl of Buffer AW2 and centrifuge for 3 min at 20,000 x g (14,000 rpm). Discard the flow-through and collection tube.
Transfer the spin column to a clean 1.5 ml or 2 ml microcentrifuge tube.
Elution: Elute the DNA by adding 200 µl of Buffer AE to the center of the spin column membrane. Incubate for 1 min at room temperature (15–25°C). Centrifuge for 1 min at ≥6000 x g. Optional: Repeat this step for increased DNA yield.

🔗 Official QIAGEN Blood & Tissue Handbook







Routine protocol to evaluate DNA concentration and chemical purity (absorbance ratios).

Turn on the computer and launch the Nanodrop software. Complete the routine initialization sequence and create a new workspace session (save the session file in your specific directory).
Pipette 2 µl of blanking solution (water or the exact buffer used for DNA elution) onto the pedestal and select “Blank”.
Assign the appropriate sample ID in the software interface, and load 2 µl of the extracted DNA or PCR product sample.
Select “Measure”. Repeat for subsequent samples, making sure to gently wipe the optical sensors with a clean, lint-free laboratory wipe between each reading. (Note: If the DNA kit performed correctly, the absorbance curve should show a distinctive peak near 260 nm).
Export the generated report as a PDF/XML file or save the current working file.
Upon completion, close the application, clean the sensors by applying 5 µl of dH₂O, lower the sampling arm, let it rest for a few minutes, and then dry the sensors.




For highly specific, high-sensitivity quantification (such as double-stranded DNA tracking), we utilize the DeNovix DS-11 FX Spectrophotometer / Fluorometer.
Unlike standard UV-Vis absorbance methods, fluorometry relies on fluorescent dyes that bind selectively to target molecules. This prevents the overestimation of DNA concentration caused by co-purified RNA, single-stranded oligonucleotides, proteins, or free nucleotides. This precision checkpoint is a critical requirement prior to downstream processing in advanced applications like Next-Generation Sequencing (NGS).
🔗 DeNovix DS-11 FX Specifications and Resources

title: Contact
text:
If you’d like to get in touch, feel free to reach us through any of the channels below.


📧 Email contact
waterhyacinthgenomics@gmail.com

title: Institutions
text:
Institutions

  



Coordinators

Dr. Alejandro Sosa - Fundación para el Estudio de Especies Invasivas (FuEDEI) & Consejo Nacional de Investigaciones Científicas y Técnicas (CONICET), Argentina.
Dr. Andrés F. Sánchez Restrepo - Fundación para el Estudio de Especies Invasivas (FuEDEI) & Universidad de Buenos Aires (UBA), Argentina.
Megan Reid, PhD - University of Florida Fort Lauderdale Research and Education Center, USA.
Melissa Smith, PhD - Invasive Plant Research Laboratory (USDA), USA.
MSc. Miguel A. Caicedo Baltodano - Fundación para el Estudio de Especies Invasivas (FuEDEI) & Consejo Nacional de Investigaciones Científicas y Técnicas (CONICET), Peru/Argentina.
Prof. Julie Coetzee - Deputy Director of the Centre for Biological Control at Rhodes University, South Africa.
Ana Faltlhauser - Fundación para el Estudio de Especies Invasivas (FuEDEI), Argentina.



Collaborators
Our work is empowered by an international network of researchers and institutional partners across the globe.

Global Collaboration Network
